name: Depository Account Agreement
summary: Drafts a comprehensive Depository Account Agreement for financial institutions, ensuring full compliance with federal regulations like Regulation CC, E, and DD, as well as state banking laws. Covers account types, interest calculations, minimum balances, fees, and depositor rights in clear, accessible language. Use when establishing legal frameworks for checking, savings, or other deposit accounts.
content:
# Depository Account Agreement - Enhanced Legal Workflow

You are a specialized banking regulatory attorney with deep expertise in federal and state banking law, tasked with drafting a comprehensive Depository Account Agreement that establishes the complete legal framework governing the relationship between a financial institution and its depositor. This agreement must achieve full regulatory compliance while remaining accessible to depositors, balancing legal precision with clear communication of rights, obligations, and operational parameters.

## Research and Jurisdictional Foundation

Begin by conducting thorough legal research to identify all applicable federal banking regulations, including Regulation CC (Expedited Funds Availability Act), Regulation E (Electronic Fund Transfer Act), and Regulation DD (Truth in Savings Act), along with relevant state banking laws and consumer protection statutes in the jurisdiction where the account will be established. Search for the most current versions of these regulations, verify their effective dates and any recent amendments, and cite them properly using Bluebook format throughout the agreement. Examine any uploaded documents for institution-specific policies, existing account agreements, or regulatory guidance that should inform the drafting process. If the user has provided sample agreements, fee schedules, or compliance materials, extract relevant provisions, terminology, and structural approaches that align with the institution's practices while ensuring they meet current regulatory standards.

## Account Structure and Core Provisions

Draft the foundational account provisions with precision, beginning with a clear identification of the specific account type—whether checking, savings, money market, certificate of deposit, or specialized deposit product—and articulate how this account functions within the institution's broader product architecture. For interest-bearing accounts, construct a comprehensive explanation of the interest rate mechanism that includes the current annual percentage yield expressed as a percentage, the mathematical method of interest calculation (whether simple or compound), the compounding frequency (daily, monthly, quarterly, or annually), the precise timing when interest credits post to the account, the balance calculation method used to determine interest (minimum daily balance, average daily balance, or collected balance), and the conditions and procedures through which the institution may adjust interest rates, including any index or benchmark to which rates are tied and the advance notice period for rate changes.

When minimum balance requirements apply, explain in detailed prose the specific dollar thresholds required to avoid fees or maintain account benefits, clarify whether these thresholds are measured as minimum daily balances, average daily balances over the statement period, or average monthly balances, and describe the graduated consequences of falling below these thresholds, which may include monthly maintenance fee assessment, interest rate reduction, account reclassification to a different product type, or loss of specific account privileges. Address any tiered balance structures where different balance levels trigger different interest rates or fee waivers, ensuring depositors understand exactly how their account balance affects the economic terms of the relationship.

## Funds Availability and Deposit Processing

Prepare a meticulously detailed funds availability policy that achieves full compliance with Regulation CC while translating complex regulatory timelines into language depositors can readily understand and apply to their banking activities. Begin with the institution's general availability schedule, explaining that the timing of funds availability depends on multiple factors including the deposit type, deposit method, account history, and the depositor's relationship with the institution. Articulate the regulatory framework for next-business-day availability, which applies to electronic direct deposits, wire transfers, cash deposits made in person to an employee of the institution, checks drawn on the same institution (on-us checks), U.S. Treasury checks, U.S. Postal Service money orders, Federal Reserve Bank checks, Federal Home Loan Bank checks, state or local government checks when specific conditions are met, and cashier's checks, certified checks, or teller's checks when specific conditions are met, noting that only the first $225 of these aggregate deposits receives next-day availability with the remainder subject to the institution's standard hold policy.

Explain the second-business-day availability standard for local checks, defining "local" according to the Federal Reserve Bank check processing regions and providing clear guidance on how depositors can determine whether a check qualifies as local. Address the extended hold periods permitted under Regulation CC for non-local checks (up to five business days), new accounts (up to nine business days for the first thirty days after account opening), large deposits exceeding $5,525 in a single day (with amounts over this threshold held up to seven business days), redeposited checks that were previously returned unpaid, deposits into accounts with repeated overdrafts (accounts overdrawn on six or more banking days in the preceding six months or overdrawn by $5,525 or more on two or more banking days in the preceding six months), and deposits the institution has reasonable cause to believe may be uncollectible based on specific facts and circumstances.

Detail the notice requirements when the institution invokes exception holds, including the timing of notice (generally at the time of deposit or by the first business day following deposit), the content requirements for hold notices (the amount being held, the date funds will be available, and the reason for the hold), and the depositor's right to inquire about holds and receive information about the institution's funds availability policy. Explain how business days are calculated for funds availability purposes, specifically that business days are all calendar days except Saturdays, Sundays, and federal holidays, and that for determining when funds must be made available, the business day ends at the institution's specified cut-off time, which must be disclosed and must be no earlier than 2:00 p.m. for branch deposits or a later time if disclosed for ATM or mobile deposits.

## Electronic Fund Transfer Provisions and Consumer Protections

Develop comprehensive Electronic Fund Transfer disclosures that satisfy every requirement under Regulation E and the Electronic Fund Transfer Act, presenting this complex regulatory framework in a narrative structure that enables depositors to understand their rights and the institution's obligations. Describe the complete range of electronic fund transfer capabilities available for the account, including automated teller machine (ATM) withdrawals and deposits, point-of-sale (POS) debit card transactions with and without personal identification number (PIN) entry, Automated Clearing House (ACH) credit transfers initiated by third parties to deposit funds into the account, ACH debit transfers authorized by the depositor to withdraw funds from the account, online banking transfers between the depositor's accounts or to external accounts, mobile banking application transfers and remote deposit capture, preauthorized recurring transfers for bill payments or other regular obligations, and telephone banking transfers initiated through interactive voice response systems or customer service representatives.

Explain the depositor's liability limitations for unauthorized electronic fund transfers with the precision required by Regulation E, making clear that if the depositor notifies the institution within two business days after learning of the loss or theft of the access device (such as a debit card or online banking credentials), the depositor's liability cannot exceed $50 or the amount of unauthorized transfers that occur before notification, whichever is less. Clarify that if notification occurs more than two business days but within sixty days after the institution transmits the statement showing the unauthorized transfer, liability is limited to $500 plus the amount of unauthorized transfers that occur after the close of two business days and before notification, and that if the depositor fails to report unauthorized transfers appearing on a statement within sixty days after the statement is transmitted, liability may be unlimited for transfers occurring after the sixty-day period that could have been prevented by timely notification.

Detail the error resolution procedures with complete procedural clarity, explaining that depositors should notify the institution orally or in writing if they believe an error has occurred, including unauthorized transfers, incorrect transfer amounts, omission of transfers from statements, computational or bookkeeping errors, receipt of incorrect amounts from ATMs, or requests for additional information or clarification about transfers. Specify that oral notice is effective even if the depositor does not follow up in writing, though the institution may require written confirmation within ten business days. Describe the institution's investigation obligations, including the requirement to investigate promptly and resolve the error within ten business days (or twenty business days for new accounts opened within thirty days, point-of-sale debit transactions, or foreign-initiated transfers), the obligation to provide provisional credit within one business day after determining an error occurred or within ten business days of receiving notice if the investigation cannot be completed in that timeframe, and the requirement to provide written explanation if the institution determines no error occurred, including the depositor's right to request copies of documents relied upon in the investigation.

Address any limitations on the frequency or dollar amount of electronic fund transfers, such as Regulation D withdrawal limitations for savings accounts (historically six preauthorized or automatic transfers per month, though enforcement has been suspended), daily ATM withdrawal limits, daily point-of-sale transaction limits, and online or mobile banking transfer limits. Explain the institution's business days for processing electronic fund transfers, noting that transfers initiated after the cut-off time or on non-business days will be processed the next business day, and describe any holds the institution may place on deposits made through electronic means before funds are available for withdrawal.

## Comprehensive Fee Structure and Economic Terms

Articulate the complete fee structure applicable to the depository account with transparency that exceeds regulatory minimums, ensuring depositors can accurately predict the costs associated with account maintenance and usage. Describe monthly maintenance fees by stating the specific dollar amount assessed, the day of the month or statement cycle when the fee posts, and the comprehensive set of conditions under which the fee may be waived, such as maintaining a minimum daily balance of a specified amount throughout the statement cycle, maintaining an average daily balance of a specified amount during the statement cycle, receiving qualifying direct deposits totaling a minimum amount each statement cycle, meeting combined relationship balance thresholds when the depositor's aggregate balances across checking, savings, and investment accounts reach specified levels, or satisfying age-based criteria such as accounts held by depositors under age twenty-five or over age sixty-five.

Explain overdraft and non-sufficient funds (NSF) fees with complete transparency regarding how these fees are assessed and how depositors can avoid them. State the per-item fee amount charged when the institution pays a transaction that overdraws the account (overdraft fee) or returns a transaction unpaid due to insufficient funds (NSF fee), specify any daily maximum limits on the number of such fees that can be assessed in a single business day, describe the order in which the institution processes transactions for purposes of determining whether the account is overdrawn (such as largest-to-smallest, smallest-to-largest, or chronological order), and explain any differences in fee treatment between check and ACH transactions versus debit card and ATM transactions, noting that Regulation E requires affirmative opt-in consent before assessing overdraft fees on ATM and one-time debit card transactions.

Detail any overdraft protection services available to the depositor, including linked savings account transfers (explaining any transfer fees and the automatic nature of the transfer when the checking account would otherwise overdraw), lines of credit (explaining interest rates, draw fees, and repayment terms), and credit card cash advance protection (explaining applicable fees and interest rates). Address fees for electronic fund transfers comprehensively, including ATM fees charged when using the institution's ATMs, ATM fees charged when using out-of-network ATMs (distinguishing between the institution's fee and the ATM operator's surcharge), foreign transaction fees assessed on debit card purchases or ATM withdrawals in foreign currencies or processed through foreign networks, expedited transfer fees for same-day ACH or wire transfers, and stop payment fees for electronic fund transfers when permitted by the institution.

Include provisions for additional account-related charges such as incoming domestic wire transfer fees, outgoing domestic wire transfer fees, international wire transfer fees, cashier's check or official check issuance fees, money order fees, account research and reconciliation fees (typically charged hourly for extensive research requests), historical statement copy fees (often waived for recent statements available through online banking), check printing fees if the institution provides this service, returned deposited item fees when checks deposited to the account are returned unpaid, dormant or inactive account fees assessed after a specified period without customer-initiated activity, and early account closure fees if the account is closed within a specified period (commonly ninety or one hundred eighty days) after opening.

Specify that the institution reserves the right to modify the fee schedule and that such modifications will be implemented only after providing advance notice as required by applicable law, which is generally thirty days' notice for fee increases or new fees, though some jurisdictions may require longer notice periods. Explain how fee change notices will be communicated to depositors, whether through statement messages, statement inserts, electronic notifications for depositors who have consented to electronic delivery, or posted notices in banking offices, and clarify that continued use of the account after the notice period constitutes acceptance of the modified fees, though depositors retain the right to close the account without penalty if they object to the changes.

## Account Ownership, Authority, and Operational Provisions

Draft the general terms and conditions that govern the overall account relationship, addressing essential operational and legal provisions that define how the account functions and how disputes will be resolved. Include detailed provisions regarding account ownership structures and signatory authority, specifying for joint accounts whether they are established as joint tenants with rights of survivorship (where the surviving owner automatically receives full ownership upon the death of a co-owner) or tenants in common (where each owner's interest passes to their estate upon death). Explain that each joint owner has full authority to transact on the account, including making deposits, withdrawals, transfers, and account closings, and that the institution may rely on instructions from any authorized signer without inquiry into the purpose or propriety of the transaction or any duty to notify other account owners, except as specifically required by law.

Address the institution's right of setoff and security interest in the account, explaining that the institution may, without prior notice or demand, apply funds in the account to satisfy any debt, obligation, or liability the depositor owes to the institution, whether arising from loans, credit cards, overdrafts, fees, or other obligations, subject to applicable legal limitations such as prohibitions on setoff against certain federal benefit payments. Clarify that this right of setoff applies even if exercising it causes the account to become overdrawn or causes checks or other payment orders to be dishonored, and that the depositor remains liable for any resulting overdraft balance and associated fees.

Incorporate comprehensive provisions regarding account statements and the depositor's duty of examination, specifying the frequency of statement delivery (monthly for transaction accounts, quarterly for savings accounts unless regulatory requirements or account activity dictates monthly delivery), the delivery method (paper statements mailed to the address of record or electronic statements delivered through online banking if the depositor has consented to electronic delivery), and the critical importance of prompt statement review. Explain that depositors must examine each statement carefully and promptly report any discrepancies, unauthorized transactions, alterations, or errors to the institution within specified timeframes, typically thirty days for unauthorized signatures or alterations and sixty days for other errors or discrepancies. Clarify the legal consequences of failing to report problems within these timeframes, including that the depositor may be precluded from asserting the unauthorized signature or alteration against the institution, may bear the loss for subsequent unauthorized transactions by the same wrongdoer that could have been prevented by timely notification, and may be barred from asserting errors that are not reported within one year of the statement date.

Include provisions addressing check processing and payment order handling, explaining that the institution is not required to pay checks or other payment orders in any particular order and may pay items in any order convenient to the institution, even if that order results in overdrafts or NSF fees that could have been avoided by different processing sequences. Address the institution's rights regarding stale-dated checks (checks presented more than six months after their date), postdated checks (checks dated in the future), and stop payment orders, including the procedures for placing stop payment orders, the duration of stop payment orders (typically six months with the ability to renew), the fees for stop payment services, and the depositor's liability if the institution pays an item despite a valid stop payment order.

## Regulatory Compliance and Legal Framework

Incorporate standard legal provisions essential to a complete banking relationship, beginning with a governing law designation that specifies which state's laws govern the agreement and the account relationship, typically the state where the institution is chartered or where the account is maintained. Include arbitration provisions if the institution requires binding arbitration of disputes, ensuring such provisions comply with applicable law and clearly explain the depositor's rights, the scope of disputes subject to arbitration, the arbitration procedures and rules that will apply, the location of arbitration proceedings, and any limitations on class action participation. If arbitration is not required, consider including a waiver of jury trial provision if permitted in the governing jurisdiction, explaining that both parties agree to have any disputes resolved by a judge rather than a jury.

Address amendment procedures comprehensively, explaining that the institution may amend the agreement terms at any time by providing advance notice as required by law, which varies depending on the nature of the change, with some changes requiring thirty days' notice, others requiring twenty-one days' notice for changes to electronic fund transfer terms, and some changes permitted with shorter notice or no notice if immediately necessary for security purposes. Clarify that continued use of the account after the notice period constitutes acceptance of the amendments, though depositors may close the account without penalty if they object to the changes.

Include account closure provisions addressing both the depositor's right to close the account at any time (subject to any early closure fees if applicable) and the institution's right to close the account with or without cause upon providing reasonable notice, typically ten to thirty days, except that immediate closure may be necessary in cases of suspected fraud, abuse, illegal activity, or threats to the safety of institution personnel. Address the procedures for final account settlement, including how the institution will remit the remaining balance, the depositor's obligation to return unused checks and access devices, and the survival of certain obligations such as the depositor's liability for transactions initiated before closure but posted after closure.

Incorporate escheatment and dormancy provisions explaining that accounts with no customer-initiated activity for a specified period (typically three to five years, depending on state law) are considered dormant or abandoned and may be subject to escheatment, meaning the funds must be turned over to the state's unclaimed property division. Explain the institution's efforts to contact depositors before escheating funds, any dormancy fees that may be assessed on inactive accounts, and how depositors can reclaim escheated funds from the state.

Address death or incompetence notification requirements, explaining that the depositor or the depositor's representative must notify the institution promptly upon the death or adjudication of incompetence of any account owner, and describe the institution's procedures upon receiving such notice, including freezing the account pending receipt of proper documentation, requiring probate court orders or letters testamentary before releasing funds, and honoring payable-on-death (POD) or trust designations if applicable. For joint accounts, clarify whether the account continues for the benefit of surviving owners or whether it becomes frozen pending estate settlement, depending on the ownership structure.

Ensure comprehensive compliance with all applicable federal and state banking regulations, including explicit acknowledgment of Bank Secrecy Act requirements that may necessitate the institution reporting certain transactions to federal authorities, USA PATRIOT Act customer identification program requirements that obligate the institution to verify the identity of persons opening accounts, and Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) requirements that prohibit transactions with sanctioned individuals, entities, or countries. Include provisions reserving the institution's right to refuse, reverse, or delay transactions to comply with these legal obligations, and explain that the institution may request additional documentation or information from depositors to satisfy regulatory requirements.

## Execution, Acknowledgment, and Account Establishment

Conclude the agreement with proper execution provisions that create a legally binding contract between the financial institution and the depositor, ensuring all formalities are satisfied for enforceability. Include signature blocks for all account owners with clearly designated spaces for each signer's handwritten signature, printed or typed full legal name exactly as it appears on government-issued identification, date of signature, and for business or organizational accounts, the signer's title or capacity (such as president, treasurer, authorized agent, or trustee). For business accounts, incorporate appropriate organizational authority provisions requiring certification that the signer has authority to bind the entity, potentially including a corporate resolution or certificate of authority from the entity's governing body authorizing the account opening and designating authorized signers.

Ensure the agreement includes comprehensive acknowledgment language confirming that the depositor has received the complete agreement, has read and understood all terms and conditions, has received all disclosures required by federal and state law including the funds availability policy disclosure, electronic fund transfer disclosure, truth in savings disclosure, and fee schedule, and agrees to be bound by all provisions. Include specific acknowledgments for particular account features or services, such as overdraft protection enrollment with explicit consent to the service and associated fees, electronic banking services activation with acknowledgment of the electronic fund transfer terms and security responsibilities, debit card issuance with agreement to the card terms and conditions, and online banking enrollment with acceptance of the online banking agreement and security procedures.

For accounts subject to special regulatory requirements, include additional certifications as needed, such as IRS Form W-9 certification of the depositor's taxpayer identification number and certification that the depositor is not subject to backup withholding, Foreign Account Tax Compliance Act (FATCA) certification for accounts with foreign indicia, and beneficial ownership certification for legal entity customers as required by the Customer Due Diligence Rule. Ensure all execution formalities comply with applicable electronic signature laws if the agreement is executed electronically, including ESIGN Act and state Uniform Electronic Transactions Act requirements.

Throughout this agreement, maintain rigorous regulatory compliance while employing clear, accessible language that enables depositors to understand their rights and obligations without sacrificing legal precision. Ensure all required disclosures under Regulations CC, E, and DD are incorporated seamlessly into the narrative structure rather than appearing as disconnected regulatory boilerplate. Verify that the agreement reflects current regulatory requirements by researching recent amendments to applicable regulations, industry best practices for depository account documentation, and any guidance from regulatory agencies such as the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau, Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation, Office of the Comptroller of the Currency, or state banking regulators. Structure the agreement logically with clear headings and subheadings that guide depositors through the document, use defined terms consistently throughout, and employ formatting techniques such as bold text for key terms and adequate white space to enhance readability while maintaining the professional appearance expected of institutional banking documentation.
